But from our perspective, particularly because we’re buying traffic, we’re buying attention, when we pay for attention, we want the attention that we pay for to be of a certain quality so that it’s worthwhile for us. And we’re willing to pay a lot more for it. That’s just a decision that we’ve made. Every day, we sit down to a buffet of consequences of our actions. And traffic is one of those areas, how you get a lead has consequences. And just be aware of them. That’s all we’re suggesting. Because you will be subject to their effects, and subject to understanding those effects as you improve your business. That’s all. That’s how I think about it.

[André] Yeah. Yeah, I’ll just reiterate what you said, that everything leading up to an opt-in is very intentional. I definitely wouldn’t stick opt-ins in a sidebar where there’s no context. Definitely wouldn’t do exit pops. I think that’s dumb to get somebody onto an email list. Maybe an exit pop that talks about a recipe that’s awesome, like a new recipe, and it leads to the recipe, and then maybe at the end of the recipe could be an opt-in. But popping up for opt-ins, and sidebar opt-ins, I don’t think that’s the way to go for the reasons that Shawn’s already articulated.

[Shawn] And then Anthony asked the second, we got this question so many different ways. It seems like 1,000 different places, so I’m really excited that we get to, I hope answer this question. So the quote, he quoted us again, an insight is not just helpful information. So here’s the question. Could you give us an example or two? I understand insight is certainly on a higher level than helpful information, but what’s the level above insights? If we give away insights for free, what’s the thing that’s even better and customers really need to pay to acquire? I’ve thought about this one, I’ll jump in first. Okay, I made this up, I think. I’ve since heard other versions of this, so maybe I didn’t make it up, and I think I did, but it doesn’t really matter. So I used to teach Google analytics primarily to clients so they could figure out what they were looking at. And I would teach analytics in a three part framework. Data, information, and insights. Data is just when you see a number.

1,000 people came to the website this month, that’s data. Information is when there’s some context with that. So 1,000 people came to the site this month, but the same month last year, 500 people came to the website. Okay, well that’s information, right? 500 more people came here, we doubled the number of people year over year who came to our website in this particular month. That’s information. And then where insight happens is when you can explain why that thing happened, in a way that’s actionable, or informative. So we might look and say like, oh, you know, okay, if we had 500 more people came to the site this year, and then you realize, well, that’s not meaningful because it was this goofy bot traffic from some Russian bot farm, so that doesn’t really mean anything. Well, that’s not an insight. But you might look and see, wow, this article that we published two years ago just got a bunch of organic search traffic, and our website didn’t get more traffic, this one article got a lot more traffic.

And then you start to ask yourself, why did that resonate with people? Now you’re getting to the level of something meaningful that might guide action. Like, well, maybe we should expand on that, or maybe that should be the focus of some lead generation, because that seems to have attracted some attention. So that’s kind of the framework. So here are some examples that we’ve shared. I’ll give you two examples. The first one, this is information we’ve shared with our audience, which is that over the 40 plus years that Andre and I have been in this business, we’ve never seen a causal relationship between a tactic and an outcome, right? We’ve never seen a particular funnel, particular method, a particular thing that says that this thing guarantees this result. That’s just information, right?

We took it to the next level though, and we said, because of that observation, the way we approach marketing is from the perspective of stacked probabilities. So even though we know there’s no specific thing that guarantees results, we have observed lots of decisions that in total, can create better results. Well, that’s an insight. The insight is like, oh, okay, I’m not in the looking for the magic bullet business. I’m in the avoiding mistakes and making better decisions business.

The person who internalizes that, a lot changes for that person, because the person who gets the a-ha moment from that, that insight that, oh, I don’t have to go out and constantly be on the lookout for the one thing that’s going to change my business, I just need to pay attention to the things that each sort of stacks the deck in my favor, and in aggregate, that improves my business. That’s an insight somebody would get from, that’s a distinction. Second example, we’ve used already in this conversation many times. This one I think is more obvious. Dean Jackson’s observation that 15% of people who buy within two years buy in the first 90 days.

That is just information, right? It’s a piece of information. But when you realize that there’s four times the financial opportunity in an audience after 90 days, and you then change everything about how you do business, because of that observation, that’s an insight. In many ways, the Art of Email in its entirety is an insight based on the realization that relationships matter, right? Like, that’s at its core. Some people might say, oh, that’s just information. Okay, we can split hairs all day long with this, but the a-ha moment someone can have that they can act on, because of a different understanding of the world that they’ve internalized, that to me, I think is a pretty decent example of our definition of an insight. Andre, what are your thoughts on this one?

[André] Yeah, I can’t better that. I mean, you’ve nailed it. In fact, right on module one, customers are credit before they buy anything. That’s an insight. It says everything. And yeah, so that seems to be one of our most popular strategic upstream decisions, because it’s such a powerful insight. And once you hear it, once it’s been unpacked, and you internalize it, you can’t unhear it. And then suddenly it just shows up everywhere. Which is very different to just a piece of information.

[Shawn] Moving onto the next question, this is from Amanda. I’m keen to hear any book recommendations you have around world building to add to my morning reading routine. I’ll let you start on this one, Andre, I only have really one. Ish.

[André] Yeah. The caveat with world building is it can be complicated, it can seem complicated and it’s easy to-

[Shawn] The master of understatement. Oh, I love that.

[André] It can be very overwhelming. I mean, there’s a world building, well, it’s partially a world building masterclass on masterclass.com. That’s mainly for people that are writing fiction, but you can use those as takeaways. One that I’ve seen more recently, in fact, it was unpacked this year by a employee of Spotify. So I’ll just post the link inside of the chat, and I’ve made sure that I’ve selected everyone. But the word of warning is, this is deep, deep, deep end, it’s not ideal for most people, because it’ll just be too overwhelming. But you know, Amanda, you’ve asked, so read that.

I don’t know, it’s maybe a five part, long form essay. Well, it’s five essays that make up this idea of world building, as expressed by Alex Danco, who’s an employee of Spotify. He’s a smart guy, and he’s done a lot of research on that, on what world building means in the business at Spotify, what they do, what he does for them. And he’s just basically used his website as an opportunity to share his thinking on it, and to better understand what world building means. So it’s a very deep, deep article. So just be careful when you read that.

[Shawn] So my recommendation for world building first would be from, you asked for books, so I would read several of Michael Lewis’ books. I would start with The Premonition. It’s his book about sort of the lead up to COVID-19, the pandemic, and sort of this very small group of people who, had they not done what they did in a very unofficial, largely obstructing capacity, probably millions more people in the United States would have died. I mean, it’s a profound story. I would also read The Undoing Project, Michael Lewis’ book. Really anything by Michael Lewis. The high speed trading one, that was a good one. But the reason I suggest Michael Lewis is because, first of all, it’s narrative non-fiction. I mean, he’s writing about real things, real people, incredibly interesting.

But he builds worlds that we can understand from almost the moment you start reading the book. Like he’s putting you in real places with real people who are really interesting, communicating oftentimes, complex ideas, that coalesce. The characters are parts of a larger whole, and throughout the telling of his stories, the larger hole reveals itself. Often, explicitly, sometimes, implicitly. But just through the power of the written word, and really good characters, he creates worlds that the reader inhabits so thoroughly. I can’t read a Michael Lewis book after about noon, because I dream about it all night. I inhabit that world. And he’s an incredible writer as well. So I would read, I think I would start with The Premonition. There’s a handful of characters. The Undoing Project is just wonderful.

Just ridiculously wonderful. But read a few of those. And I go back, and I look and see how I, I’ll go read a chapter, like, what the hell did he do here? When he first introduced a character, how did he introduce the character? Why, by the end of that chapter, do I feel like I know this character, and I really understand her, and have empathy for her, and I’m interested in what happens next, and I sense something big is coming, I sense she fits into a larger hole? How the hell does he do that? The challenge with Michael Lewis’ books is, they’re so good that you have to read them cover to cover, but, for me, I wouldn’t want to deconstruct it on the first experience. Read it, experience it, go back, and then dissect.

Like, what has he done? And then, the shorter answer, that’s not a book answer. This is a question. I love questions. When you’re thinking about world building, the one question to ask yourself all the time is, what else? That’s the question. So, you’ve created something. Whatever, you’ve got a landing page, what else? Well, if you’re doing a sphere of influence inspired front end, you might have this coalescing belief that you want someone to accept before they’re ready to opt in the right people, and that maybe you have to make sort of four or five points before you get there. So you’ve kinda figured that out, but now they’ve opted in, they’ve raised their hand and said, I’m the right person. Well, what else? Well, now they need to understand this. Okay, what else? Well, then they need to understand this.

Great. Eventually, when you answer, what else five or six times, you’ve got a relationship building series. Well, great, well, what else do we need? Well, there’s this concept that, it’s three parts, great. Start creating the three parts, and once those are done, and now you have the three pieces of content that are sort of publicly visible, and a relationship building series, and an opt-in, well, what else? Well, now, they’re sort of thinking maybe I have a sense, ’cause where’s my audience relative to purchasing something from me? Okay, well, maybe you have a soap opera series where you’re leading to some sort of hand raising. Okay, great, what else? World building is always the answer to the question, what else? And you never, ever, ever stop answering that, because every time you create something new, and this is the fundamental most profound insight of the Art of Email that I don’t know that we’ve articulated well yet, ’cause it’s hard to articulate in the written word, much easier to articulate in the spoken word, which is at the center of the Art of Email is this profound idea that the moment you introduce one new thing into the system, the entire system changes.

It is not linear additive. You’re like, oh, I have a landing page with seven emails, and so I have an eight part system, and when I add the next thing, it’s one more layer, it’s now nine parts. That’s not how systems theory works. The math, and what we talked about in the Art of Email enrollment series, about how we had rewritten one particular email over and over again, I wrote the base for the email that was just ridiculously bad. It was bad, not because the idea was bad, it was the expression of the idea was bad. This is what I was trying to share. Systems theory tells us it’s not the parts, it’s the relationship among the parts. And any time you add a new part, you change the nature of those relationships. The example I used is I worked with a well-known internet marketer for a period of time who scaled his business from four people to 16 people in one shot.

And in his mind, it failed miserably. And the reason it failed was because conceptually, we think that a 16 person business is four times more complex than a four person business. 16 is four times greater than four, right? But a systems theory tells us that a 16 person business is 20 times more complex than a four person business, because of the number of potential relationships among the people. When you have a three person business, you have four people who have relationships with three other people, right? That’s how it works. When you have a 16 person business, you have 12 people who have relationships, I’m sorry, you have 16 people who have relationships with 15 other people.

The math gets crazy. So bringing this back to the Art of Email, when you’re world building, every time you introduce one new piece of content into the system, the entire system changes, because there’s a new relationship in the system. So if you have initially, landing page, seven part relationship building series to value newsletter, that’s not a very complex system. But the moment your value newsletter, you create a new piece of content by asking and answering what else, the whole system has changed. ‘Cause now you have this other entry point into the system. This is such a profound idea that it’s hard to capture in words, but this is at the core of the power. You have the potential for exponential benefit with the Art of Email, because the entire thing is built to work together as a system.

All right, I didn’t mean to talk about that, but, okay. Let’s move on to the next thing. This one has tremendous amounts of background. So this is about world building. So the question is for the politics part of world building considering who has power in the market, and how it’s exercised and displayed. I’m uncertain what to consider as my market. Now I’m gonna summarize what Matt has shared with us here, ’cause it’s a lot. Matt, his market is a geographic place, Alaska. His audience are people who are interested, my sense is that particular place. He has a website with a photo journal, he wants to sell photos, visitor consultations, people were coming there already. You know, I’m just looking through this to make sure I cover the basics so we all understand it. I think that’s most of it.

So his question really is, when you have this hyper local, no real competitors doing what he’s doing, how do you think about politics, is this idea in world building, because politics focuses on who has power in the market, and how that power is exercised and displayed. I’m going to give a very short answer to this question, and then Andre, I’ll hand it over to you. But the short answer to this question is, that part of world building is not relevant to what you’re doing. That’s a simple answer. So skip it. Politics, we included that in our conception of world building, because it’s impossible to think about our prospects outside of the context of other players in the market.

So Andre and I have consulted with some of the larger financial publishing companies. When you work in that market, you can’t forget that overwhelmingly, people in that market are sold to a very specific way. What they see in their inbox and in front of them every day has a certain character to it. And when we think about how we want to approach that market, we don’t do it in a vacuum. We have to think about it in the context of what they’re seeing all day, every day, knowing that influences what we’re showing them. So that’s why politics matters. For us, lots of people in our market, they’ve created language around funnels, and trip wires.

We can’t pretend that that language doesn’t exist. We can’t pretend that our audience isn’t exposed to that. So for us, politics is really recognizing that’s out there, that’s real, and we need to be aware of it, so that when we are engaging with our audience, we’re recognizing that the world that they are in includes some things that we need to speak to, even though we don’t believe in them, recommend them, et cetera. Anything to add on that, Andre? That’s kinda a weird question, hard question.

[André] Yeah, it’s a hard one. I’ll add that, you can also think laterally about this. Somebody that’s gonna go to Sitka, Alaska is a traveler, presumably, somebody that likes a certain type of scenery, or place. Well, I’m pretty sure that somebody that visits your little city in Alaska has visited other cities and other countries, right? So as a traveler, what is the market saying and doing?

These people that travel to your neck of the woods, or would like to travel to your neck of the woods, where else do they go? How are those people presenting their stuff? What are they saying, what are they doing? So you can glean some insights from that, and then use that to inform some decisions about your little place in the world. That’s the only thing I can add.

[Shawn] Next question’s about, a couple more about world building as well. So, first one, how do you know it’s time to go from a town to a city, and what does that process look like? I wouldn’t conceptualize it that way. We’re putting artificial names on a thing with a comfortable metaphor. We understand conceptually the difference in size between a town and a city, right? But the way the question’s written, don’t don’t think about it that way.

Like, just sit down and create one piece of content. Write a paragraph, or a sentence, whatever. And that sentence will turn into something else, and use the what else framework, or whatever, just create your content, and you’ll wake up one day, and you go, oh, there’s a lot here. Maybe that’s a town, maybe somebody else would think it’s a village, maybe someone else thinks it’s a metropolis. Who cares? Just create. I don’t see a lot of value in thinking about the process of going from one size to the other. Maybe I’m missing the point of the question. Andre, do you have a thought on it, I feel like I’m not seeing where that would lead to a valuable insight, I may just be missing it.

[André] Well, I think that the reason why we expressed it that way is someone that’s not doing any world building, or they’re just starting out, and they don’t have any world as such, but one of those final things. So it’s easier to start small. So that could be a little three page, multi page thing that people go down this little journey that’s three pages deep, that’s part of world building, and it’s a very small one. But when you start by looking at something that’s huge, with hundreds of pages, and ask yourself the question, well, how can I build that? That’s not productive. You get there by expanding something that starts small. That’s all you’re expressing in that idea.

[Shawn] I’m gonna move onto the next question. This is from Guillermo as well. Do you have a template or a framework for writing a manifesto? Why don’t you answer this? I’m curious how you would answer this before I start running my mouth.

[André] The short answer is no. Other than a framework which I think we may have shared before. I’m just trying to think now if we have. But it’s really just how to think about deciding on what’s gonna be inside of any long form piece. So I’ll have to check. But, actually, we definitely have spoken about it inside of the Momentum Building Workshop, as part of writing a landing page.

– There, and we also have a simple writing framework. Yeah, simple writing framework on the site has parts of it. I mean, I think that the other answer to this, I’m glad I waited for you to answer, because I was just gonna be like, no, move on, right? Which isn’t a great answer. Only because I hate templates. This idea of a template makes me wanna scratch my eyes out. Here’s a better answer. Well, here’s the caveat. I write everything like a prosecutor. I’m always making a case. I make the lead, you can sort of see in the writing that we do, you can kinda see where I show up once in a while when it feels a little hard-edged, because that’s just how my brain thinks. I’m taking you on a journey, and at the end of that journey, this is what I want you to believe. And in order to believe that, then I need to make the case for it. And I make the case in a way that to me, feels consistent, and rational, and logical, and step-by-step. I don’t know how to do it a different way.

So I give you that context, Guillermo, so that you know, when I answer this question, I’m answering only from my perspective. And the way I think about writing a manifesto is that there is something at the end of that manifesto I want you to believe. It is the controlling idea that I want you to understand, internalize, and accept if you’re the right person, right? The right person meaning you’re a high-quality prospect, or whatever. It’s usually a sentence. It’s not 10 paragraphs, because then, what’s the point? It is a thing, right? So, that controlling idea, in order for that to be true, I then ask myself, what do you need to believe, in what order, so that this thing that I want you to accept at the end is the logical conclusion of the case that I’ve made?

Now how I make that case may not be that cold and precise, but when you get there, when you get to the end, I want to have a very clear idea of what I want you to believe at that point. And I want to have done the work to have made an airtight case, so that when you get there, you’re nodding your head. If you get all the way to the end, and you’ve accepted each of the things that I’ve said, that you’re nodding your head saying, this makes sense, and it coalesces to this point, where you go, yup, that’s it. That is exactly it. Let’s do a quick shout out for Amanda who’s on this call, Amanda Bond.

So Amanda has probably one of the best sphere of influence front ends I’ve seen. I’m thinking about your Facebook ads, so it’s not a traditional sphere of influence front end, and I don’t even know if it still exists, and if it does, I don’t know if you wanna share a link to it, if it doesn’t, sorry for opening a loop for everybody who can’t see something. But Amanda had this really great, she made such a compelling case for why the way most people were doing Facebook advertising just didn’t make any sense. The results sucked on and on and on and on. But she made such a compelling case that by the end of it, if you reached the end of it, all you wanted to do was figure out, what should I do now? Because it just coalesced around this point that you couldn’t help but share her worldview when you got there.

It was brilliant. It’s so, so brilliant. It’s such a perfect example. That’s the feeling that you want. It’s hard. And there are other examples too, I’m just seeing that Amanda’s on the call, I really wanted to point that out, ’cause I’m thinking about that. That’s the feeling that you want with a manifesto. And again, lowercase manifesto. A manifesto, it’s just us sharing our opinions about something. The manifesto for the Art of Email is longer, it’s more involved, I think it’s 2,200 words or so, where we did a couple of interesting things with that, where we have characters who we re-introduce at a different point, that are metaphorically related to the larger point. So we did a couple of things there that were nuanced, but at the core of that manifesto, was a controlling idea.

And that by the time you get to the end of it, we want you to have internalized that controlling idea. And we want you to feel like we made the case, an airtight case that when we get to the end and we say something, you don’t go, wait a minute, where the hell did that come from? That what we have said at the end is, it feels like, right, of course. That’s what an insight feels like. Is when you sit back from your chair, and you go, damn it, of course, that’s why I haven’t been getting the results I’ve been trying to get, because I’ve been doing all these things that make perfect sense if this is true, but damn it, that’s not true.

So, that’s an insight, of course. So go, Amanda, share the link, go read that, that Amanda created. And you’ll feel what insight feels like. And you’ll also feel what a really good manifesto, lowercase M manifesto feels like. Anyway, all right, I’m gonna move on to the next question. Do you, wait, you answered that one first. All right, next question from Guillermo. This is about world building, the last question about world building, shockingly. Would you say that second order thinking must be present in every step, or only a focus in a bridge email? I cannot wait to hear, actually, we’re talking about bridge emails, no, we’re not gonna answer this one. We’ll answer this in the bridge email upcoming, oh, go ahead, answer it, Andre. What the hell. Sometimes I think have brain damage.

[André] Yeah, second, third, fourth order thinking should be your default stance. You know, the world doesn’t operate on first order thinking, in my opinion. I mean, everybody does it. That doesn’t mean it’s the right way to look at the world. The world is complex. There’s consequences to every single decision, and consequences to those decisions. And it can go on and on and on. And every single decision that you’ve made has been a result of something that you’ve been exposed to, that’s mostly probably subconscious, and other things that are conscious, that you’ve just forgotten about. And it’s all effecting things. So, yeah. We all need to think in second and third and fourth order. That’s my short answer.

[Shawn] Thinking should be second order and beyond, always. First order thinking doesn’t make any sense. We need to go through first order thinking to get to everything else. But every decision that we make, we need to think about the second, third, fourth order consequences. We can get too analytical with this, we don’t wanna do that. But, again, it gets back to some of the examples we shared earlier.

If you give away free stuff to get leads, you have leads that like free stuff, right? Like that’s no different than if you use Facebook’s algorithm to get leads, you’re getting people who generally sign up for stuff. Just be aware of the second order consequences of where you get your traffic, how you get your traffic. But all of that has consequences. It’s not good or bad. It just is. And second order thinking helps us better understand what is. That’s all. All right.

Wow, we’re making progress, we’re already at the strategy module. Okay. This was a fun one. Actually, these next two are fun ones. So this is from the strategy module. Anthony asked, he quoted us again. It’s funny, every time someone quotes us, I always feel like there’s an shoe about to drop. I don’t know why I read it this way, but I always read a quote where it’s like, you idiots said this, now it makes no sense. I don’t think you lack here, but I always feel that for some reason. All right, so Anthony quoted us saying, our vision can be summarized as a simple idea of marketing with integrity. So then he asked, so it’s not create happy customers.

If that’s the case, what would you call create happy customers? A goal, or a business objective? And then the next question is, also, often the visions we hear about a lot sound more tangible and measurable, such as the founder had a vision 10 years ago that his business will become a Fortune 500 company in X years, with X billion sales. So I guess visions don’t have to be tangible and measurable. I’m gonna let you answer this one first, Andre, because I feel like I’m gonna rant. And I hope that you modulate my inclination to rant.

[André] Should I answer the first part or second part?

[Shawn] Okay, first question is if that’s the case, basically, he’s asking, is it marketing with integrity, or is it create happy customers? What are those two different things? So that’s the first question.

[André] Right, so, our business objective is to create happy customers. That’s the thing that we’re in business for. And the definition of create happy customers is something that’s special and dear to us. Our definition will be different to somebody else’s definition. And we do that through marketing with integrity, empathy led marketing. Those are the vehicles in which we create this thing, or create or generate a happy customer factory. Yeah. I can’t wait to hear your rant, though.

[Shawn] Maybe it’s not a rant. So, the goal of TLB, as a business system, is to produce happy customers. That’s how we know the thing is doing what it’s supposed to do. We can measure, yes, happy is subjective, there’s are some objective measures, but it’s largely subjective. But that is the goal of the business system. We know if the business is doing better or worse, based on the rate at which it’s producing happy customers. Straightforward. The way we do that is marketing with integrity, which is both things that we’ll do, and a shit ton of things we won’t do. So we’re not gonna pressure people into buying stuff, we’re not gonna try to sell people who shouldn’t buy our stuff. There are a million things we won’t do, and a million things we won’t say. So when we talk about marketing with integrity, that means something very specific to each of us. And it’s shocking that we both have exactly the same view about what that means. It’s sort of what makes our collaboration work so well.

One of those things, for example, is, this is the way to think about this, the vision, where does this fit in? We will not teach you something that we don’t do. We won’t tell you to do something we don’t. Period. Because we think that’s integrity. You might look at that and say, that’s stupid. If it works, why wouldn’t you teach it to us? Well, because we don’t think for us that that represents integrity. So, it’s an amorphous thing. I’m trying to see the question behind the question, like, how can you use what we’re saying to be helpful to you? So maybe I’m searching for something that, you know the answer to that question, I don’t need to. So for anyone listening to this, the goal of your business, maybe it’s the vision too, and we can be splitting hairs. And I’m not sure the value of that hairsplitting.

So I think I’ll just stop doing it, and move on to the next question. So the next question was, often the visions we hear about are something a lot more tangible and measurable, such as the founder had a vision 10 years ago, blah, blah, blah. So the question is, I guess visions don’t have to be tangible and measurable. Well, I’ll answer this one first. Your vision can be whatever you want your vision to be. And if it resonates with you, that’s all that matters. If we wanna think about this in a functional term, a vision creates contrast, and contrast creates tension, and that tension pulls us forward, right? So there’s contrast between the vision you have for a desired future, and where you are relative to that vision right now. And if that vision is attractive, the idea is that that structural tension, Robert Fritz has worked around structural tension, that future reality is so exciting it pulls you toward it. If that’s true, then the only vision that matters is the vision that lights you up, and creates enough contrast that it pulls you, or creates enough tension that it pulls you toward it. I’ll give a personal example.

I don’t know if this is meaningful to anybody else. But I have a strange relationship with money. For me, I am not motivated by money. I don’t sit down every day and look at sales, I don’t do that, I’m not motivated by money. My relationship with money is that I want to make enough money that I don’t have to think about money. Is that a vision? No, I don’t have a vision board in my office, where there’s a picture of me and a thought bubble, and it has money in it, andd there’s a red circle or an X. That’s not part of my vision. But internally, I know that when I look out at a future, and I think about the success of that, an ingredient in that is, I don’t want to think about money day to day, month to month.

I just don’t want it to be on my radar. I don’t enjoy thinking about that. That’s not a very compelling vision. It’s not tangible. It’s not measurable. I mean, how do you measure not thinking about money? I don’t know. So I think, to me, it just returns back to the central idea that, I don’t know that somebody else can help you, or that somebody else’s definition of vision is a good fit. I mean, maybe for you, you know, you might look at this and think to yourself, you know what, 10 years from now, this business that I’m creating, and a lot of people do this.

I wanna create this business that impacts 100,000 or more people through X, Y, Z, and make $100 million dollars at it. And if you see that every day, and that motivates you, and that lights you up, and your world is better as a result, hell yeah, man. Run with that all day long. So don’t let anybody, especially us, suggest that our vision should be your vision, or how we think about vision should be your vision, no. You find what lights you up, and chase that every single day. I think that’s the best answer I can give to that question. Andre? Vision? Tangible? Measurable? Andre has a vision board, I bet. You do, don’t you?

[André] I don’t. Yeah, I don’t think that many businesses start off thinking that they’re gonna build a billion dollar thing. I think it starts off with founders, or a founder wants to disrupt the status quo, or do something meaningful in the world, and it just so happens that it turns into a billion dollar business.

I don’t think the two Airbnb founders, I’ve read their story, I don’t remember the details, but I don’t remember that there’d ever been them having a conversation that they’re gonna build this billion dollar property sharing, rental sharing thing. It started off as a photography idea, and they just wanted to, again, I don’t remember what their vision was per se, but it definitely wasn’t to build a billion dollar version of what Airbnb is today. It started off smaller than that. So, yeah, I don’t have a better answer than that. I think exactly what Shawn says. Whatever it is, just go and do it. If you wanna build the billion dollar thing, then go make it happen.

[Shawn] All right, we’ll move on to the next question. This is under the module, How to Think About Writing. Another question from Anthony. Quoted us twice. Double the fun here, I know we’re in trouble. First quote, we’re writers, not copywriters. Can’t remember where we said that, I just remember that we did say. This isn’t a writing module, imagine that, he actually told us that. Then quoting from the manifesto, email marketing hasn’t been around for a very long time, but it still has plenty of established wisdom. Most of those ideas were inherited from old school mad men like Claude Hopkins, Eugene Schwartz, and David Ogilvy.

Now the question is, if we deliberately ignore the buy, buy, buy, now, now, now bit, are there any golden that people can still learn from these old school guys? Some established copywriters still insist that studying their work will create miracles, especially if you hand copy their many proven ads on a daily basis. Or did you mean we should not stop paying attention to their work completely? All right, I’ll jump on this one first. Okay, let’s look at what we said, first of all. We’re writers, not copywriters. Never said you shouldn’t be a copywriter. If copywriting lights you up, if you get a positive effect from the principles of copywriting, sure. Use them. Just be aware. this is where we get the second order consequences. Copywriting is designed for one specific purpose, which is to get people to buy.

That’s at the core of copywriting. So when you use principles of copywriting, you are using principles that are engineered to get people to buy right now. That’s not opinion, that’s fact. That’s what Claude Hopkins and Eugene Schwartz did, and Ogilvy, and many others. And that’s what copywriting is about at its core, is how to take someone from prospect to customer, in the traditional definitions of the term. We never say don’t do that, but this idea, and this is the point that we were making, that if the only way you know how to communicate with an audience is through the single lens of copywriting, then you have this person with a hammer syndrome, which, the adage is, it’s the law of the instrument, which is to a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail.

Better phrased, to a person with a hammer, everything looks like a nail. If you embrace copywriting as the way that you write, and copywriting is a style of writing to get people to buy, everything that you write using the principles of copywriting will sound like you’re trying to get people to buy. That’s just the logical conclusion of that. And when we say that overwhelmingly, we think there’s power in building relationships first, copywriting is not a method of writing that builds relationships, it’s a method of writing that creates sales. So we think there’s a better way to write to build relationships. More conversational, more natural, more accessible to our audience. And sure, if you wanna use principles of copywriting when it’s time to sell, if that works for you, sure.

Some of our best friends are copywriters. Barry’s still on the call, Barry and I are gonna spend all afternoon with truly, some of the best copywriters in the world. There’s nothing wrong with copywriting. It’s just not the only way to write. And for us, 15% of the content we create has any relationship to selling. So, to have all of our writing influenced by copywriting principles, it just wouldn’t make any sense for us. It wouldn’t make any sense for the way we teach, either. So, long answer, short-ish question. But there’s nothing inherently wrong with copywriting, just like there’s nothing inherently wrong with a sledgehammer. But if you’re hanging a picture in your living room, probably don’t wanna use a sledgehammer. All right, Andre, take it away.

[André] Yeah, I think it’s just two very different styles. And I think if someone is too indoctrinated in the one, so for example, copywriting, and their only lens into their world is through copywriting courses, copywriting books, and they work in an environment that’s full of world-class copywriters, it’s difficult to think in any other way, Joe runs one of the Agora affiliates. And he was at a seminar that we hosted many years ago in Miami. And afterwards, over a drink, he was telling me that the hardest thing, these guys are world class copywriters, they write world class copy all the time.

And he says that the hardest thing that they found was trying to get their copywriters to write emails. Nothing would work. Their style of writing just expressed through an email, they just couldn’t get that working. And so, yeah, I mean, if you’re a copywriter, and you enjoy copywriting, just write copy, in that way. If you wanna be both, well then learn both. For Shawn and me, we could care less about reading direct response copy, just so that we can understand it, and then write more like copywriters. That’s not how we wanna write. So, that’s it, really. What about you? Anything else to add?

[Shawn] Yeah, I mean, I think the point to close on with the copywriting thing, there’s nothing wrong with copywriting. It’s an instrument. Use it. If it works for you, great. If comes back, works for your audience, even better. Just be aware, when you use principles of copywriting to write all of your copy, it’s going to have a downstream consequence. And when that consequence arrives, don’t be surprised by it, that’s all. That’s it. All right, Karma asks, let’s see. We have a few questions in here, this is about writing, but he snuck in some things about relationship building series and soap opera series. Nice work, Karma.

It’s really a question about writing, so we’ll let it slide here. Question number one, when writing your emails for your relationship building series, or soap opera series, or really any other email you write, how much editing do you do? This is gonna be so much fun to answer. Do you use a checklist of things to look for and do? I know when I do copywriting, I use a checklist to help make it better copy. Do you use one, and when do you stop editing, and not let it get out of control trying to be perfect? Oh, that’s a really good question. We’ll answer the second question next, that’s different. So, what’s the best way to tackle this, Andre? Do you wanna talk about how you write, I’ll tell you about how I write? How do we come at this, and answer the question at the same time, in a way that’s valuable to everybody?

[André] Yeah, I don’t know. I think the meta idea is that there is no one way that’s gonna, my style of writing, at least how I start to write is very different to Shawn’s. Well, I’ll let Shawn express how he does it, but for me, I’m a discovery writer. So the very first thing I do is I need to write what I call a draft zero, which is just me getting the stuff out of my head. And then once I’ve done that, I can then create my shitty first draft. And I’ll then take the ideas that revealed themselves on the page from my draft zero, and I’ll start to construct something that has more structure.

But I don’t worry about anything to do with structure when I’m just vomiting my draft zero onto a page. And that’s just what I need to do to get the stuff out of my head. Yeah, otherwise it just rattles around inside there. And then I do the shitty first draft. And then shortly after that, I’ll edit it to something that I’m happy with. And that doesn’t have to be a long process for me. So before Shawn came along, and I had to do this all myself, some of the emails I wrote, or the stuff I wrote was just a one version thing. It just came out, it was more or less the way it needed to be, I felt, and I would just do some spell checking, still get that wrong, and then send it out.

Whereas now, Shawn and I approach this slightly differently. So sometimes, when structure’s maybe more important initially, Shawn will write a lead, or he’ll start it off. I’ll still write my draft zero on the side, just so I can express my ideas onto a page. I don’t have to share that. And then I’ll then edit what Shawn gives me, or vice versa. I’ll send Sean a version that I’ve written, which is normally one order deeper than my draft zero. And then he will then look at it, and say to himself, what, this doesn’t make sense, And he will restructure it and make it nice. So, yeah, that’s my answer.

[Shawn] I mean, probably the most shocking thing in our relationship is two people who write as differently as we do, write as seamlessly as we do together. It doesn’t make sense, it shouldn’t make sense. And I think it speaks to two things. One, shared values sort of make everything else more effective, but also, there’s no right way to write. We write so differently that the idea that there’s a way to do this is insane. So the way I describe our two writing styles is Andre generally writes to find a lead, I write from a lead. So I have to figure out, though, I say he’s Anne Lamott, I’m John McPhee, right?

So I have to figure out the structure first. I’ve tried just write by the seat of my pants, shitty first draft. I’d rather kill myself than ever do that. I hate it. Hate it. For me, I write a lead, and that lead has to be a certain level of precision. It’s my foothold into the writing. And then I write. And what I will do is I feel like I’m running ahead, I don’t know, 100 yards. And then at a certain point, my energy begins to diminish, and I go back to the beginning, and I read what I’ve written already, edit slightly, and that propels me through the next 100 yards. And I build a piece that way. I read, and reread, and edit.

After graduate school, I was a journalist for a year, so of course, as a journalist, you write from a lead, the phrase don’t bury the lead comes from journalism, right? So for me, like often, I will read the base piece that Andre has written, and I will find that, usually, between 40 and 60% of the way there, the lead for what Andre wrote has revealed itself in his writing. But for me, I couldn’t have written the first 60% or 40% without knowing what the lead is. So very different styles of writing. So, specifically to answer your questions, how much editing do you do? We do a lot. We edit very differently. For me, editing and writing are not separate processes, there’s one process. For Andre, they’re separate processes. Is one better, one worse? Nope, they’re better or worse for each of us. Do we have a checklist we use?

No. Well, maybe. We have an unconscious checklist in our minds because we’ve been writing for decades each. But there is no checklist that’s like, okay, open loop, check, cliffhanger, check, PS, check. No, we use none of that. There’s nothing to suggest that there’s anything wrong with that. And certainly, I could see that being a very effective framework, like pulling ideas out of the principles of serialized narrative, and having sort of a checklist of things, like have I done these things? Do I have a controlling idea? Do I know what I’m writing about? Have I mapped that value to the audience?

Do I know the emotions I want my audience to experience as a result of what they’re reading? Yeah, no reason that couldn’t be a checklist. There’s wrong with that. The last question. When do you stop editing, and not let it get out of control trying to be perfect? Well, the Art of Email was scheduled to be delivered in March, and we shipped it in October. So, I don’t know, Andre, do you think we have any problem with being perfectionists? Do you ever get that sense when we’re writing.

– Not at all, no.

[Shawn] God, we’re awful about it. Here’s the caveat. We enjoy that. There’s a precision that works for us. And we’re recovering perfectionists, lowercase R for recovering. Please don’t try to write perfectly. It’s a road to nowhere. Perfect is the enemy of done, perfect is the enemy of good. You’ve heard all those phrases, just get something that’s 80% quality out the door, right? Spend 20% of the time, 80% quality.

And the last half of that efficiency curve is not not in your favor. All right, next question from Karma, Andre, what are your personal thoughts, yeah, okay, I remember this one. What are your personal thoughts on how often you should mail your audience? I know Ben Settle likes daily emails. I know everyone, business owners will have their own opinion and likes on how often, what are your thoughts? Let’s do short answers to this one, just because we’re gonna cover this in later modules. But what are your thoughts on how often you should email your audience, Andre?

[André] Very broadly, when someone’s first coming into your world, so through a relationship building series, which we’ll cover next week, I think that those emails should be daily. ‘Cause you certainly wouldn’t wanna email somebody brand new once a week. So daily, to a point when they now know you. They know who you are. They know your email. When it hits the inbox, they expect it, they anticipate it. And then how you decide to rig up your daily newsletter is completely up to you. As you said, Ben Settle does daily. That would be torturous for me. Ben and I have laughed about this many times before, over a drink.

And that just lights him up, he loves writing daily stuff, and that’s kind of his business model, is those daily entertaining emails that he just loves writing. I couldn’t do that. There’s just no way I can do that. So, our business, which is an expression of how we wanna show up, can’t be that way. I mean, other than sequences that people go through, you would never receive newsletters from us that are daily. That’ll just never happen. But that’s not to say that it’s wrong. I mean, you can write daily newsletters all day long. Yeah, that’s it.

[Shawn] Yeah, this question, I’m chuckling, because one of the people on the call, Julie and I, she used this phrase, that’s one of my favorite phrases, which is the, how long is a piece of string question. Who knows? How often should you email your audience? What’s the context? What’s their level of interest? Is your relationship with your audience that you’re a fundamental part of their profession? That’s true often for Ben Settle’s audience, and you want communication every day, is there sort of inspirational, they get it in the morning, and off they go? Sure. I mean, that makes perfect sense.

But, how often is that the case? I have a reaction to this, and I don’t know Ben at all, I’ve never met him. I know who he is obviously, and I know generally his philosophy, as it’s public facing. But I have had more conversations with clients who have said to me, well, I follow Ben Settle, and I do daily emails to my list. And I just look at them and think to myself, in what world could you ever have imagined that your audience, relative to what you do, is something that you should communicate with them every day? Like, it makes no sense whatsoever. And I think, in general, I think it is very unusual, measured over the course of months and years, that our audience wants to hear from us every day. Yes, it can work in certain circumstances in certain markets.

But, that, to me, that’s very rare air. I think it’s much more effective to have some level of humility, and say, where do I fit in my prospect’s world, and what is the frequency with which I, like, what makes the most sense for them? And maybe that’s just the wrong way to look at it. You know, it might be a better way to look at it, but, that’s just my reaction to it, I don’t know. And again, my reaction is colored by the number of conversations I’ve had with people who have said something to me that just appears at face value, intellectually indefensible. So, I’ll put all of my context out. All right. Next question from Abe. This is a question for a later module, so we’re gonna skip it.

We’ll move it to the appropriate place for next week’s call, I believe. Guillermo asks the question, all right, so how do you figure out the best formatting to make things readable? Do you find what works for you in the same formatting? Have you thoughts on that, like formatting, templates, guidelines for editing, do you have thoughts on that? I don’t have anything to contribute to that question.

[André] Yes and no, I guess. I’ve always been somebody that loves white space, and minimalism. So the website, you know, TLB is an expression of that. The emails that we send look like that. There’s nothing wrong with fancy looking emails and ones that are inside of a nice looking template, if that’s what your audience likes, and that what makes you happy then go ahead and do that.

Our template, it’s just a slightly modified version that you get bog standard from ConvertKit, which is the ESP that we use. And it’s responsive, so it looks nice on a mobile phone, and there’s no distractions, and that’s it, really. So the thing that shines is the text, is the message. There’s no distractions. No colors, no nothing. I don’t really have a better answer than that.

[Shawn] Yeah, I don’t have any answer for that. I mean, it’s written content, can somebody read it? Can my mom read it? I forward emails to my mother once in a while. Hey, look what Shawn’s up to these days. If my mom comes back, and says, I can’t read this, then there’s a problem. That sounds ridiculous. But it’s like, as long as the message is able to be consumed. I mean, that’s probably a stupid answer, let’s pretend I didn’t say that.

All right, let’s go on to the next question from Guillermo. This is the nine point puzzle section. Says I’m pulled in two directions when I read this. Everyone tells us to understand our avatar and research, et cetera. Yet, if you think of the nine point puzzle, you’re saying to not be trapped by your assumptions, how do these two concepts fit together when building all this out? I think you’ve made a leap here, right? Maybe. Maybe I’ll talk myself into making the same leap with you. But, the nine point puzzle is really just this recognition that when you expand the edges of what you’re looking at, more solutions become available to you, right?

And unspoken assumptions can trap us to seeing fewer opportunities. So this idea of, to understand your avatar and your research there’s no reason to be trapped by that. I mean, yes, you should understand what resonates with your audience. And we’re not suggesting that that’s going to somehow lock you in. I’m not seeing the relationship between those two things. So, I think the way I would approach having you think about this is to say, look at your avatar, research your audience, but allow yourself to put that in a broad context. For example, recognizing that your audience can only tell you what they know that they want you to tell them.

They can’t tell you what they don’t know that you know that you can tell them. So the nine point puzzle would suggest that when you’re thinking about creating a world for your audience, that you don’t limit that creation only to what they can tell you that they want, that you also need to include the things that you know that they don’t know, because they don’t have the ability to tell you about something they don’t know that they want. That’s really an application of the nine point puzzle, I think here. Anything to add to that, Andre? I have no idea if that made any sense.

[André] Yeah, it does. And I think the point of the nine point puzzle wasn’t about listening to what the market wants and giving them this thing. I don’t know of anybody that comes into someone’s world and says, oh, I wish that was a sales funnel, I would have felt better about it, but it’s not a sales funnel. And then they email you.

[Shawn] I just laughed out loud when you said that, I muted myself. Sorry to interrupt you.

[André] This is more meta than that. This is the stuff that you do, external to surveying a market, understanding what they want, and then expressing that in a way that’s beneficial to them and to you. It’s two different things.

[Shawn] I mean, think about it this way, too, right? Like, I mean, from the perspective of the nine point puzzle, if we decided to create an email course on the assumption that if you don’t ask people to buy, they won’t, everything that followed, like the course will be radically different. The recommendations would be radically different, if we just had that assumption. But the moment we challenge that assumption, the possibility space expanded as a result. All kinds of new opportunities become available to us. Now we don’t just embrace them because like, oh, look, this seems possible.

Yes, we need to pair that with, okay, what does this mean? We have to actually look at it. But the nine point puzzle just tells us to expand our possibility space when we look for things. That’s really I think, all that it’s saying. I am super psyched, we just finished all the written questions. I thought for sure, there are like 10 more pages. So now, we’ll jump over to the hand raised questions. I’m going to do these in order, I think.

That’ll be the easiest way, or most respectful way. All right, Karma asked the question, do all of your sales come from inside your email? I love this question so much. Do all of your sales come from inside your email marketing with Art of Email? For example, is it traffic, multi page, presale site, email sales, or do you also do things like sell via webinars? I’m trying to imagine me convincing you that we should sell with a webinar. That’s why I’m laughing at this question. I’ll answer this question, and then hand it over to you, just ’cause I’m the jackass laughing here about it. Yes, we use paid traffic to generate awareness.

The Art of Email is a new course, even though it replaces an old course, when we sell what we consider a new course, we do that only to our audience. There were no affiliates, nothing else followed. So for this particular example, in general, broadly speaking, we bring people into our world, we build relationships with them, and we point them in different directions, and we let them self-select to raise their hands, and the things that they’re interested in, they buy when they want to buy. That is how we sell. We do not use other methods of selling. Again, this gets back to marketing with integrity. We can’t disparage webinars and the buy, buy, buy now stuff, and then also do that thing. So, no, we do not sell by webinars. But look for our course on webinars, just kidding, we don’t do webinars.

Nothing wrong with webinars, by the way, it’s not a value judgment. A webinar’s a vehicle. If you sell with webinars, you think, wow, that wasn’t very nice, don’t hear that. It’s comical to me because it’s not something that is within our realm of how we would do something. And that is not a judgment about the thing itself, it’s just about how does that fit into the world that we’re creating, and a webinar, as of now, does not fit into that world. Not saying we would never do it, but I think the probability of us selling by webinar is low. We’re both writers too. So we have faces for radio. So probably not doing webinars. Andre, you want a run at that question?

[André] Yeah. You know, every mechanism or vehicle, and there’s many, there’s an opportunity cost with it. It requires attention, and resources, and backend complexities. It just adds to that. And for us, at least how I’ve always wanted to show up, is, I love just writing an email. So pretty much everything you’ve ever seen from me, since the beginning of time, has always been text-based. So the world building, the sphere of influence inspired stuff, that’s all text.

But there’s no reason why you can’t use video for that. Or a hybrid of video or text. It’s just for us, well, initially for me, I’m a writer, and then when Shawn and I hooked up, that was a great fit. And obviously, email. And we link the two together, and that’s how we show up. I mean, somebody can come to our website now, brand new, and hit the buy button and buy. They don’t have to go through the stuff. Once they buy, they’re taken through a journey in the same way as a subscriber is taken through a similar journey, they just haven’t purchased a product. And then they’ll come in through a different avenue.

And we then do, as you know, what we’re busy teaching inside of Art of Email. But again, it’s all powered by email at that point. And then we could use webinars, we could do podcasting, we could do all sorts of other things. It’s just, there’s an opportunity cost, and that’s not how we enjoy sharing information. But there’s nothing wrong with it.

[Shawn] Wait till you see my ideas for Black Friday. Webinar power. Joking. I just like to screw with Andre, for all of you who are like, really, are they gonna be webinars? No, probably not. Open loop right there. Okay, next question, Cara, did I pronounce your name correctly? How do you strategically manage your energy when it comes to serving all customers under your care, versus serving those who have purchased, bought into your inner world? Example, dedicating energy to answering customer’s personal questions, and the context is a tiny team, serving the dating and relationships niche.

This is such a good question. When everyone is a customer, how can, should you distinguish those in the inner world from the outer, in terms of value given, if at all? Or is the reward treasure from a customer perspective, mostly in the value of the product itself? Why don’t you tackle this one first? That’ll give me a moment to see if I can figure out an answer.

[André] I think the simple answer is we don’t really distinguish. We show up, and we share, and emails that we get that end up in Help Scout, we don’t go and check if someone is a prospect or a customer, and then respond, we just respond. We just, in that moment, if it’s a long email, it normally gets pushed aside just for a bit longer, because there’s an opportunity cost, and a time constraint, but we will get to every single email. But yeah, we don’t go check. And if it’s a customer, we answer differently. Context matters. So if the question is about becoming a customer, then that’s important to know. If it’s already a customer, and they’re asking a question that’s inside of a course, that’s different. But I think for the most part, people just engage with us, and we engage back.

And our outer world is the stuff that we share on the website, some of it is the stuff that we share, and our inner world, at least the way that I see it is, our inner world is as soon as somebody gets onto email. So even they become a customer. That’s a version of an inner world. They’ve taken the commitment, and raised their hand, and opted in, and they wanna know more. So at that point, they’re inside of our inner world. And then there’s obviously different layers to that, because at some point they’re gonna feel pulled to invest in one of our courses, and then that’s even deeper down the rabbit hole.

But again, we interact with those people in exactly the same way. In many cases, somebody that’s asking about a product, so they’re obviously inquiring about something that they don’t quite understand, we will give a long email. I mean, Shawn even hits record on Zoom, on Loom, and we’ll riff for 40 minutes, and then send it to them. And that’s not a customer, that’s just how we’ve chosen to show up. And we don’t really distinguish what we’re gonna say, other than being the most helpful. I think that’s my answer.

[Shawn] Yeah, and everything you said is what I would say, so I don’t have much to add other than to say, I think there are a couple of things to consider here too. And we are deliberately, I don’t wanna say building because Andre built this business, and then I came in later, and we’re building something new together. So I don’t know what the proper term for that evolution would be, but we believe in craft, right?

So we want the ability to be able to interact with our audience, customers, whatever, paying, not paying. Again, this is a second order thinking thing. If we had a million customers, we couldn’t do that. And just to be clear, if you get an email that has our name on it, we wrote it. I know lots of people, and this is not meant to be disparaging, but I know lots of people out there in our little corner of the universe, they have people who write as them. And you get an email, and I had that experience once of interacting with someone who I thought I was interacting with, based on the signature on the email, only to find out later, when that person and I became friends, that he had never seen those emails, we had never communicated. And that was really, really gross. Really gross.

And he’s a great person. It’s just, it was a business decision. So for us, we have made this decision, again, for us, we think about this as being in integrity, but when you get an email from one of us, one of us wrote it. When you get one of my rambling, 45 minute Loom videos, I record it, that’s harder to fake, that’s me. But I think there are a couple of layers within this. One, this is actually easier. It’s a lot easier to not distinguish paying customer versus, like, I don’t wanna do that. Like someone sends an email, and then I’ve gotta go look into their customer record, and, screw that, that’s no fun. I never wanna have a business where I have to do that. So that’s a decision we’ve made about how we want to experience the world. So lots of nuances here. It’s easier, it’s more authentic to who we are.

It does have limitations. We occasionally feel bad, because we can’t be as responsive as we want to be. Like over the 10 days of the Art of Email enrollment, it was hard to keep up sometimes. And there were times when I felt like I wish I had responded sooner than I eventually did. And I didn’t wanna give the wrong impression to the person. So it does have consequences, but on balance, we’ve just made this decision that we want to have a business where we can interact directly with our audience. It’s, from my perspective, always beneficial, I always learn something in every interaction with every person.

I’m endlessly fascinated by people. And I recognize that someone’s giving to me their most precious resource, which is their attention, that I wanna reflect back the respect I have for having received that attention. So it just feels like this beautiful dance of life that’s going on when that happens. It doesn’t feel like a burden in any way. 99% of the time. Sometimes we get dumb questions, frankly, so I don’t wanna pretend it’s all sunshine and unicorns. Okay, next question. This is Michelle. I hope I pronounced your name correctly. My clients are heterosexual couples. Usually one of them decides to buy, and enrolls the other.

How would you think about relationship building and world building, given this two headed buyer with two very different sets of pains, fears, and desires? Ooh, that’s a good question. How do you think about that, Andre?

[André] Oh, man.

[Shawn] Well, let’s think out loud about this for a second, right? So we’ve got two people, okay, one of them decides to buy and enrolls the other, but my assumption based on that, is both of them are consuming the content. Maybe. Or maybe one’s consuming the content and sharing it with the other. So that could be part of it. How would you think about relationship building and world building given this two headed buyer?

So here’s how I would think about it. Assuming that my understanding is correct, which is that one person probably has signed up for this thing, and that person is sharing it with the other, maybe forwarding the emails, that could be one thing, or more likely, having a conversation with the other person about, what do you think about this, or on and on. Then knowing that, then you’re speaking to sort of three things, maybe. maybe I’ll get the 10 by the time we finish here. So, first of all, you’re communicating an idea to the person reading. You’re also communicating an idea that the person reading is going to share with another person, which is an order of magnitude more complicated.

And you can do that deliberately. So you can make a point, and then after you make the point, essentially show how to share the point that you’ve made. That’s sort of another challenge. And it may be, depending on your audience, the way that you’re sharing the point, you have to give some nuance. I’m just imagining, just because you’ve said these are couples, you know, couples have dynamic relationships, there can be all kinds of dynamics in that.

And I’m just gonna make something up here, if this were like a relationship improvement kind of thing, the tone has to be cautious, where if someone has signed up for something, essentially raising his or her hand saying, our relationship could be improved, that that has a certain psychological vibe to it. Then that person is learning things which he or she is sharing with her partner who didn’t sign up and say, I want help. Like this is subtle, but I think it really comes down to this, if you know your audience needs to share what you’re explaining to them with somebody else, the question to ask is, how do I make what I’m sharing shareable?

So how do I explain something, and then summarize it in a way that they can explain it to somebody else, in a way that’s positive, and call attention to the possibility that when they share it, the other person might not be entirely receptive to it? So something like that feels right, if I’m understanding this, something like that feels right, ish. Any thoughts to add to that, Andre?

[André] Yeah, only a more complicated version, because, ultimately, women communicate with other women in a language, I’m not gonna articulate this well. In a certain language that guys may not get or understand, it’s just different. And whereas guys talk to each other, maybe, let’s say it’s like bar talk, it’s how you would talk to talk to your friend at a bar, and it’s different to how two women would talk when they’re enjoying a glass of Prosecco at a wine bar, compared to the guys.

I don’t know, I’m just thinking out loud here. So one option could be is there’s two paths when they enter in the business, and it’s, are you male or female, or would you rather not say? But at that point, you could have two characters, ideally, a guy that writes the guy stuff, and a woman that writes the woman’s side of the conversation. And then these two characters can still operate in the same business. So the newsletters that go out could have a piece that’s written by each person, or, I don’t know, Fridays, it’s the women’s newsletter, and Saturdays, it’s the guy’s newsletter, for example.

However, but then I think there’ll be a different level of resonance when emails are sent like that. But they’ll still be shareable in the sense that a woman will receive an email that’s deeply resonating with her, because of how another woman thinks, and then she’ll click forward and send it to her husband, or her partner and say, read this dude. And that’ll be the end of that. So, I don’t know. I think that’s another way of looking at it. It’s just, obviously, it can be a lot more complicated to set up, or to do.

[Shawn] I’m going to make precisely zero comments about gender dynamics, so I don’t put my foot right squarely in my mouth. I’m just gonna move on to the next question. All right, this is from Francis. What are some good questions to ask your audience to get the right feedback? We don’t do this a lot, Francis, well, we do, actually. I say that, and immediately, the moment I said it, I’m like, well, that’s wrong. Of course we do.

I was thinking survey questions. We ask questions that emerge from the writing. And they’re all over the place. I mean, last week’s newsletter, it was just a chance to have some fun. We’d written 20 emails over 10 days, and the Friday email, we just kinda wanted to have some fun with it. So one of the things I mentioned was, I’m sort of getting into craft coffee now, and I asked like, if anyone has any recommendations, send them our way, and got lots of recommendations. Like, is that a good business building question? Well, I mean, objectively speaking, probably not, but every person who sent me recommendations had taken enough time to take a step closer to me in that relationship, and everyone who did that is gonna get an email back from me that further builds the relationship, so was that question valuable?

It seems pretty valuable to me, right? Did it sell something? I don’t care. It’s not the point. It’s the relationship, right? I mean, that was just an opportunity to have some sort of interaction. The way you frame the question, it’s sort of like a sliver that’s catching me a little bit, which is to get the right feedback, the right feedback for what? What does he want the feedback on? So I think that’s why I initially thought survey kind of questions. So, my second favorite quote is EE Cummings, always the beautiful answerer who asks the more beautiful question. So, asking good questions is going to get you better feedback, but, what’s a good question for you, what’s a question that’s going to give you meaningful information you can act on to the benefit of your audience? So start there.

And some questions, they sound clever, they don’t work. Questions that don’t work are things like, what haven’t I shared with you that you would find to be more valuable? Well, how the hell do they know? They don’t know what they don’t know, so that’s not a great question. But you can do functional questions, which are just things like, we use these, on a scale of one to 10, how interested are you in X? And you can’t use seven, right? Remove seven. Usually the polling software doesn’t let you do this, but if you tell people not to use seven, 80% of them use seven, so. But I would still do that. Scale of one to 10, how do you feel about X? Make it very clear, don’t compound things. Don’t try to get too deep into the weeds of, they answered yes to this, and they see this. I think the way to think about this, just imagine you sit down, you have 15 minutes at a coffee shop before you’re getting on a plane, and an ideal prospect sits with you. I don’t know how you know that, but you know that.

This is an ideal prospect. And they say, listen, you know what, I’m gonna just get myself a cup of coffee, I see that I’m an ideal prospect of yours, what would you like to know? Well, what the hell would you like to know? Those are your questions. Maybe don’t ask them all at once. But ask them. I mean, it sounds like a terrible answer, but that’s the real answer. Like, there’s no magic to this. There’s no template, there’s no checklist. You’ve taken a step back, and kinda taken a couple of hours, and asking yourself, what would I really like to know? What’s something that I would like to know that would inform what I do for the next 30 days, next 90 days? What’s a question that I could ask my audience that could transform my business, depending on what they said? But have fun with it. Just start hammering out questions, and then just pick two or three of them, and ask them. If you’d ask them all at once, ask two or three. If you wanna ask them over a period of time, then write emails around the question, and then ask the question. That’s another way to do it. Andre, thoughts on this? Doesn’t sound like a great answer, but we’re coming up on three hours, so I’m not sure how many great answers I’ve got left in me.

[André] Yeah. Only just to add that, to humanize what you’re doing. It shouldn’t just be about business, or the business side of it. Like Shawn mentioned, with the coffee. We share real life stuff pretty often. We do it in the PS, we do it in other places, and people respond to that. So it’s not necessarily answering your question, but people are responding. They are sharing. And there’s a connection that’s made that can have a long lasting effect. So that’s my dumb answer.

[Shawn] All right, yeah, I think I won that one. This is from an anonymous attendee, ooh, open loop. I feel that tension, it’s anonymous. Do you normally put all of your emails on your website? Do you do this for Google purposes, or just to send people back as a reference? That’s part of world building. We, this summer, created content-based emails specifically to put those on the site.

That was just an experiment that we did. Sometimes we put email series on the site later, sometimes we write multi-part themed series, and we put those up. Yes, it’s for Google. Often, you’ll see when you’re in the course, you see us referring back to things as examples. So it’s nice to have them where people can go read them. So, I think the larger answer to your question is just, yes, we do this for a lot of reasons. But mainly it’s just, we show our work. Ad it’s helpful for us to see, when we talk about things, we can show how we do these things as well, ’cause we know that there are lots of people who come to our website, who are never going to be able to afford to buy a course.

But they’re giving us their attention, so we owe it to them. We give them something of value. So we create free courses and we show our work. And for some people, who may or may not be able to afford it, their choice, but some significant number of people are going to go see that, and take some value from that, and improve their lives, in some small or large way as a result, and that just feels like good karma to me. That’s more of it. That, and just the functionality of being able to point to it, and the little squirt of dopamine we get once in a while when we go back and read something and think, damn, we did that, that’s nice too. Like, let’s be honest. Anything I missed in that, Andre?

[André] No, that’s pretty much it.

[Shawn] Squirt of dopamine always comes up. No day goes by without a squirt of dopamine content.

[André] It’s what makes the world go round, dopamine.

– Apparently.

[André] But yeah, we’re big fans of evergreen content. And as Shawn said, if there’s stuff that we can see is gonna be valuable to others, this is also about world building, and expanding our world. So many emails are edited slightly, and then put out, posted on the website. Not everything. Yeah.

[Shawn] All right. Next question from Francis, this is a funny question from my perspective, so I’ll answer it first. There are two answers to this question. The question is, who are the best copywriters in the world? It seems everyone is these days. So the first and most important question is, our answer to this question is largely irrelevant, for a variety of reasons. We’re not copywriters, we’re not in any position to judge copywriters, we are not within the community of copywriters.

So, asking us who we think the best copywriters are in the world, that’s the context. The reason I’m chuckling about this is I’m spending the next three days, virtually, next three days with John Carlton, David Deutsch, David Garfinkel, and Kevin Rogers, who are probably four of the best known and best copywriters in the world. So, it’s sort of a present question. So I think, intellectually, arguably John Carlton, David Deutsch, David Garfinkel, Kevin Rogers are certainly among any top 10 list of copywriters anywhere in the world. And I’ve learned tremendous amounts from each of them as writers, and as just wonderful human beings.

So, my heavily influenced pick, the top four, that’s the top four. But it is so wildly biased that, don’t count on it as an answer to your question, really. It’s just, I enjoy the timing of that question relative to my next three afternoons. Andre, do you wanna take a stab at the best copywriters in the world, you’re probably more tuned in to this than I am?

[André] Yeah, not really. I mean, I don’t follow copywriters, so I don’t know most of them. I mean, I know the ones that Shawn’s mentioned, and John Carlton is an amazing writer, not just copywriter. I mean, he can tell stories. And I think he’s a storyteller before he’s a copywriter, even though he would say he’s a copywriter. He just tells amazing stories. So I think the best copywriters are the ones that can tell great stories.

I don’t know who they are. I know of John Carlton. And I’m sure there are many others. But those are the ones that would resonate with me. But then, how do you measure this? The ones that get paid the most, the ones that generate the most revenue within a context that’ll be different when that same copywriter writes for somebody else, and then it bombs, so who knows?
