[Shawn] Yeah. And someone put Gary Halbert’s name and others in the chat. I read the question as the best living copywriters, I should just have mentioned that. I wasn’t thinking about Halbert and others. All right. Next question is from Julie. All right, so Julie asks, how do you transition writing to your list from the old style? This is not the style we advocate in the Art of Email. And starting to write with intent of world building. So this is a good question. All questions are good questions, I say that, just, my reaction to it is it’s an exciting question to answer. There’s sort of two ways to think about this. One is to pull the bandaid off, right? And you just start writing a new way, there are ways to kind of transition. I think the larger question to ask yourself is where does your audience come from already? What are their expectations? And then how does that influence the decision that you’re making to switch styles? And what is the size of your audience?

How much of that audience are paying customers, like all of these questions. Because if you have a small audience, small list, you’re just getting started, you’ve been writing in one way, then I would say, just start writing in a different way. If you have an existing list, I would kind of tell people, you know, sort of sell the idea of relationship building series, and then have everybody on your list go through that series, and then on the other side of it is your Art of Email inspired approach. That’s one way to do it. And then everybody has kind of a standardized starting point, and you’ve sort of informed them what to expect. And recognize, and Julie just posted that she has less than 600 people, that some percentage of those people are not going to pay attention, either right now or ever, you’re gonna lose some of those 600, because you had a different voice before. That’s fine, that’s okay.

Conversely, you might have a lot of people sending you feedback, this is amazing, and thanks so much. I mean, who knows? You’re going to get a reaction. That’s one way to think about it. A second way to think about it is more subtle and nuanced, which is, look at how you’ve been communicating, and then choose something, maybe a soap opera series, maybe you create a newsletter, but that something, your voice is a little bit different. And that voice, I don’t wanna say new versus old, but you’re making a gradual move. And I guess a third way, maybe, there are probably 30 ways.

The third way I would think about this too, is perhaps you take a soap opera series, let’s just, for the sake of argument, say it’s 10 emails. And over the course of those 10 emails, you’re transitioning from current voice to new voice. And because that happens over that 10 email period of time, it’s so subtle that it never feels jarring to the audience. They just have sense at the end that they’re more engaged with you at the end, but they don’t really know how it happened, it just happened.

I think that’s what I would do. Not necessarily 10, but I would craft a soap opera series where the underlying intention in my mind is how do I take everyone who was on my list now, and turn them into perfect prospects or customers, however you wanna think about it. What would I need to do to craft that experience? And then make that the soap opera series, create that, and send everybody through it. So that you know on the other side of it that transformation. That’s how I think I would approach it. Andre, your thoughts on that?

[André] I’d probably write a soap opera series. Five, 10 emails. And it’ll take somebody on a journey. It won’t sell anything. It will be something of value packaged up in a serialized narrative. In your new narratively serialized e-voice. And then I would send out a bridge email, and ask people to raise their hand if they’re interested in hearing this thing that you’ve created. You could say, I’m trying something new, would love for you to send me feedback if you like it, or if you don’t like it, let me know as well. And click this link, and it’ll hit your inbox, and it’ll last five emails, 10 emails, whatever it is.

And then, that’s it. People will go through it. 600 people won’t go through it, but a percentage will. And you’ll get feedback. And it’ll either be overwhelmingly positive, or negative, or something. And if it’s positive, it’ll light you up. And you know, maybe then the next thing would be to do your relationship building series in the same tone. And that’s how you can gradually build your thing out. Sometimes it’s nice to get that squirt of dopamine.

There it is.

[André] And you can do that nicely with a soap opera series.

[Shawn] There it is, I’ve started a trend here. Never trusted the dopamine. We’re down to our last question. Ah, excellent. All right. Here’s the question, from Mary, I think? I hope I pronounced that correct correctly. With the Art of Email definition of what a customer is, how do you communicate with people who are not a good fit for you, but yet consume your content, either on social media, or end up on your email list and reply to emails? And the context is that she has a decent social media presence, and it’s starting to happen a lot, and she sometimes feels bad for not engaging the same way with those people as everyone else she considers to be a good fit.

Should I treat every follower and subscriber as a customer, even when they’re not a good fit? We deal with this a lot too, incidentally. And we get lots of people who send us emails, and through any variety of indicators, usually it’s some variation of, I wanna learn how to make money really fast, which course is the best for it? That’s usually a good indicator. But we have a fair, I don’t know, five to 10% of our inquiries are what would fit this definition. They’re not our people, they’re not our prospects. We’re not going to make them happy if we sell them something. And what we do is we just point them to our free resources. They still get a personal response, in general.

But we’ll say, and often, it’s surprising. We actively discourage people from buying a lot. Because buying for them, very clearly from what they’ve shared with us, is not the best solution that we can offer them. So what we do instead, often, is we will say, essentially, appreciate you reaching out, recognizing that they are sharing their attention, it’s important. And if it’s something like, how do I make the money the fastest? Then we’re very upfront, and say, we’re not in the make money quick business. We don’t have anything that fits that really well. However, we generally don’t wanna leave people empty handed. Say, however, if you are interested in internet marketing, we have a tremendous number of resources, including free courses, and then we’ll share a link either to the free resources section of our site, and maybe draw attention to a starting place, like, our 2020 year in review is a good place to get started. So we do engage.

We don’t just look at something, and go, ah, not a good fit, forget it. And it doesn’t scale. We can’t do this with a million prospects. But, the way our business is constructed, we have something else. And I guess the last point I’ll make on this is just because we do that doesn’t mean you need to do that. You might make a decision that to protect your attention, and for you to be able to create the most value in the world, you need to not engage with that. Particularly, social media, I don’t engage with any social media, I don’t look at comments on ads, or anything else, because despite my best efforts of not being affected by it, it irritates the hell out of me. So I just don’t engage with it. So be cautious of what you do let into your life, because it can throw you for a loop. So you don’t owe anybody that. It’s a choice that you can make. And make the choice that’s best for you, I guess, is the message. Andre, how would you approach that one?

[André] I heard Seth Godin express a version of this a long, long time ago, maybe a decade plus ago. And I loved his answer, and I’ve never forgotten it. And one of the attendees at a conference that he was at asked him the question is like, why aren’t you on social media, or why aren’t you on Twitter, or wherever he was. And his answer, which is a lot more eloquent than I’m about to express, is basically, there’s an opportunity cost of being on a place.

And he has a certain level of quality, or, he shows up, and he has a certain amount of time. And that time allows him to only do so many things. And he can’t do everything, he can only do some things. And of all those things, he would rather show up on his website, and write his daily email. And then obviously, his books, and things like that. So he’s not on Twitter, because he doesn’t have the time to be on Twitter. And he’s not just gonna be on Twitter to post little Tweets pointing to his articles, because that’s not showing up on social media in the way where you can establish relationships.

So he refuses just to jump on social media just to post links to stuff that he’s writing. If he’s gonna use social media, or use Twitter, he wants to engage with people. And he said he doesn’t have the bandwidth for that, so he doesn’t do it. So if you wanna hear about me, Seth Godin, you come to my website, you opt in, and you get my emails, and you buy my books, and that’s how you hear about me. And so yeah, I’ve never forgotten that. And I’m just gonna jump onto my high horse here very briefly. There’s many people that think just because there is social media that they should use social media. And just because social media is Pinterest, and Twitter, and Facebook, and Instagram and, TikTok, they need to be everywhere. And the only way you can really be everywhere is by putting thin pieces of stuff out there.

And yeah, so I don’t agree with that. I mean, if you wanna use social media as a channel in, pick one or two, based on how much time you can give, pick one or two where you can engage with an audience in a meaningful way, that obviously then points to your main world. And if that’s only one social media channel, then that’s the one social media channel that you should be on, and ignore all the rest, as if they don’t even exist. That’s how I would do it, and I think other people do it. And there’s people like Dave Perell, who, he’s on Twitter, he gets so much traffic from Twitter because he’s there, engaging all the time. And then he writes long form content, which is on his website, and that’s his strategy. But he’s not on TikToK, or wherever else. He’s chosen the places that he’s willing to show up, and spend the time to engage with an audience. And he gets the reward from that. Yeah, I’ll climb off now. That’s it.

[Shawn] My AirPods are dead. So, I think that I’m done speaking here. Literally like, 5%. So two things quickly, before we wrap up. One, thank you to everyone who managed to stay for three hours and 16 minutes. That is no small feat. So we appreciate your attention, our attention, as always. Next week’s content will be informed by the questions that were asked today. So there’ll be some of that. And then next week, we’re going to dive into really the meat of some of the initial modules. So we’re talking about the relationship building series, the value newsletter, and the soap opera series. And there’s a lot of ground to cover. So we will see everybody next week. Thanks, everyone.

[André] Thanks, everybody.
